channel.title: AI UX Porridge
channel.description: A daily 1-2 minute briefing on the top AI and product design news.
channel.itunes:author: Pete Rogers

item.title: AI UX Porridge - 2026-07-15
item.description:
It's Wednesday the fifteenth of July, and it's also National Hot Dog Day, so if you've got a few minutes at lunchtime, you know what to do.
First up, Apple just opened its redesigned Siri AI to everyone through the iOS 27 public beta. It's the first time they've made it widely available beyond developers, and with 2.5 billion active devices worldwide, even a fraction of those users means this is the biggest test yet of Apple's answer to ChatGPT and Claude. The new Siri can access your personal data—emails, photos, messages—and it's deeply woven into the OS now. You can call it up by saying "Hey Siri," pressing the side button, swiping from the Dynamic Island, or just searching in Spotlight. There's even a standalone app if you prefer it that way.
Next, Google Images got a major redesign that basically copies Pinterest. Instead of just searching, you now get a "For You" gallery of images tailored to your interests, updating in real time. You can save ideas to collections and browse for inspiration rather than racing toward a specific result. Google's also letting you generate images right in Search with their Nano Banana model when you can't find what you're looking for online.
Now for something design-focused: a startup called SXD just closed a $4.5 million pre-seed round to solve one of fashion's biggest waste problems—cut-and-sew waste. Between 10 and 15% of fabric gets thrown away during the cutting process, but SXD's AI software reengineers clothing patterns so pieces interlock like puzzle parts, leaving nothing behind. Crucially, it doesn't ask factories to change how they actually work—just how they cut. The company's got partnerships with H&M and Harvard and MIT's bookstores already.
Last one: Anthropic's latest ad campaign is creeping people out. It starts with a burning house, then shifts through images of surveillance, homelessness, and mining operations while a voiceover asks hard questions like "Can AI be trusted?" Sam Altman took a cheeky shot at it on X, and plenty of others agreed—the company that's trying to position itself as the ethical choice is doing it by basically saying "AI is doomed," which, yeah, lands awkwardly.
Quick toolkit check before you go. GitHub Copilot added security reviews right in the app so you can catch vulnerabilities as you code. Mixpanel now supports passkeys, so you can ditch passwords and sign in with your fingerprint or face instead.
Oh, and before I go—heard this one today. Two carpenters are doing a remodel, right? One's straightening old nails he pulled out, shaking his head and tossing some in the trash. His partner asks why he's wasting good nails, and the guy says "Because the head's on the wrong side!" The other one looks at him and goes, "You idiot! Those nails are for the *other side of the house!"
Catch you tomorrow.

item.title: AI UX Porridge - 2026-07-14
item.description:
It's Tuesday, July 14th, 2026. Also Bastille Day, so if you're feeling a bit revolutionary, now's your chance.
First up, Apple's gone to court against OpenAI. The company's alleging that OpenAI systematically poached over 400 Apple employees and used them as a pipeline to steal hardware secrets. The lawsuit is pretty detailed — Apple's got messages from ex-iPhone engineers talking about accessing confidential files, saying stuff like "LOL, I found out I can access the network storage." Apple's arguing this wasn't just rogue employees; it's baked into OpenAI's culture from the top down. The company's claiming OpenAI's Jony Ive hardware project is "rotten to its core" because it's built on stolen trade secrets.
Next one, Anthropic's localizing Claude pricing for India. It's already the service's second-biggest market after the US — about 5.8% of global usage — so the move makes sense. They've started showing rupee pricing on Claude's website and apps, though they haven't switched on UPI payments yet like OpenAI did with ChatGPT. Claude Pro's going for about ₹2,000 a month, Claude Max around ₹11,999. It's a small thing, but friction matters. And it signals Anthropic's doubling down on India — they opened a Bengaluru office earlier this year.
Now for some design news. Waze is rolling out AI-powered features built on Google's Gemini. You can now ask conversational questions to find places — "Find me a coffee shop that's open right now" — and Waze will surface options. The app's also learning your route preferences based on your history; if you like highways over local streets, it'll show those first. Plus there's a new Motorcycle mode launching in Latin America and Southeast Asia that accounts for bike-specific hazards like potholes and narrow bridges.
Last one's about the bigger picture. A piece in Fast Company's looking at how AI fundamentally changes what design means when it's woven into every layer of an organisation. The argument: taste can't be delegated. When AI can generate anything instantly, a designer's real value shifts from making outputs to knowing which outputs deserve to exist. That requires judgment, and judgment comes from exposure to exceptional work and having the responsibility to act on it. It's less about tools and more about identity.
Quick toolkit check before you go. Claude Code's got a browser now built right into the desktop app — you can pull up docs, websites, and designs without leaving the window, and it'll click through and interact with them just like a regular user would. Pretty handy for testing stuff. And before I go — heard this one today: why was the Gorgon named "Medusa"? Because calling her "Zola" would have been cheesy. Righto, enjoy your day.

item.title: AI UX Porridge - 2026-07-13
item.description:
Monday the 13th of July, and it's Embrace Your Geekness Day, so if you've got a strong opinion about GPU programming or AI alignment, today's your day.
First up, Mira Murati's Thinking Machines Lab just published a report arguing for AI that's actually shaped by the people using it. Right now most models are trained once, in a handful of places, then frozen forever. They're calling for distributed, customizable AI—giving users tools to fine-tune their own model weights, better interfaces to talk to these things, and publishing research so more engineers understand how it's all built. It's about pushing both knowledge and alignment closer to the people who actually need it.
Next one's a bit more technical. There's a new coding guide walking through NVIDIA's tile-based GPU programming—cuTile, Triton kernels, Flash Attention, the whole stack. The idea's pretty elegant: instead of writing code thread-by-thread, you operate on entire tiles of data. Load them into the kernel, compute efficiently, store results back. They've built a practical Colab workflow that adapts depending on what hardware you've got, testing everything from vector addition through to full matrix multiplication against PyTorch for correctness.
Now for some design news. UX Collective just published a piece that's been rattling around my head—"The Interface Has Left the Building." The argument's that for thirty years we designed screens. Menus, buttons, hierarchies. The desktop metaphor gave us language. But that's breaking apart right now. People are talking to software now, delegating tasks to it, and increasingly not interacting with it at all because it's already handled things in the background. Chat, voice, agentic AI—they're reshaping what UX design fundamentally means. The screen's still there, but it's not the primary surface anymore.
Last one: there's a fascinating piece about fake quotes and how easily we believe them. A fabricated Bezos quote about sacrificing human comfort for data centres ricocheted across Reddit and X in June before anyone fact-checked it. It never happened. But it landed because the feeling of it rang true, which tells us more about how we're reading AI's future than about the man himself. We're not great at verification, and that matters when information's moving this fast.
Oh, and before I go—heard this one today. Bloke walks into a men's room at a stadium, mid-piss, and somehow drops two quarters into the trough. He gets annoyed, crumples up a twenty, and whips that into the trough next to the quarters. Guy next to him says, "Why the hell did you do that?" He goes, "You don't really think I'm sticking my hand in the piss trough for just 50 cents, do you?" Righto, catch you tomorrow.

item.title: AI UX Porridge - 2026-07-10
item.description:
It's Friday, July 10th, and happy National Capybara Day — the only day we collectively agree that oversized, chill rodents deserve recognition.
Right, let's get into it. First up, Meta's making a real push into frontier AI with Muse Spark 1.1, this new coding model designed to handle complex, multistep workflows across enterprise systems. It's agentic work — bug fixes, code migrations, that kind of automation — and Meta's pricing it at $1.25 per million input tokens, which puts them roughly in line with what Anthropic and OpenAI charge. The real story here is Mark Zuckerberg posted about it on X for the first time in three years, which... probably says something about how seriously Meta's taking the AI race right now.
Next, there's a messy legal situation unfolding between OpenAI and The New York Times. The Times is claiming OpenAI hid evidence in their copyright lawsuit — specifically that OpenAI conducted internal searches of its training data and kept records of ChatGPT conversations to measure how much it was infringing on copyrighted journalism. OpenAI had been arguing they couldn't do those searches for technical and privacy reasons, but a deposition apparently showed they'd already done exactly that, with a database of about 78 million conversations.
Now for some design news. Mill, the startup that makes bins that dry and shrink food waste, is scaling up to commercial kitchens. They've spent hundreds of hours in restaurant back-of-house operations studying what gets thrown out, and their new commercial product uses AI to analyze waste and help kitchens throw out less in the first place. It's honestly one of the more practical climate-tech approaches I've seen — not fusion, just better product design making waste reduction straightforward.
Last one: Google just rolled out a new feature in AI Studio that lets you import a GitHub repository directly, then keep iterating on it in their vibe-coding environment. Build mode takes your code, transforms it into a runtime-compatible format, and you can deploy it from there. It's a small thing, but it's the kind of workflow simplification that makes these tools actually useful instead of just impressive.
Quick toolkit check before you go. Figma Make now has GPT-5.6 built in — just pick it from the model picker on all plans. GitHub Copilot got the full GPT-5.6 family rolling out across all SKUs, so Sol for heavy reasoning, Terra for everyday work, and Luna if you want the cheap option. And one more: GitHub Copilot Chat now generates repository overviews on first visit, which saves you from reading through a README that probably doesn't exist anyway.
Oh, and before I go — heard this one today. Sherlock and Watson are camping, right? Holmes wakes Watson up in the middle of the night. "Look up at the sky and tell me what you see." Watson goes through the whole bit: millions of stars, millions of galaxies, potentially billions of planets, the time is a quarter past three, God is all powerful and we're insignificant... then Holmes goes, "Watson, you idiot. Someone has stolen our tent." Get some rest this weekend.

item.title: AI UX Porridge - 2026-07-09
item.description:
It's Thursday, July 9th, and it's National Sugar Cookie Day, so go bake something.
First up — SpaceXAI just dropped Grok 4.5, and Elon's calling it an Opus-class model. The big deal here is token efficiency. SpaceXAI's claiming twice the efficiency of other leading models at $2 per million input tokens, versus Opus's $5. That's a meaningful cost advantage if it holds up in the real world. Elon's saying it's roughly on par with Opus 4.7 but faster and cheaper — the kind of combo that actually matters when your infrastructure bill is the limiting factor.
Next one — Google Photos is getting a new AI tool called Video Remix. It's powered by Gemini Omni and lets you do things like relight videos, swap backgrounds, or slap artistic filters on clips in seconds. Rolling out today to Google AI Plus subscribers in a bunch of countries. It's the kind of feature that keeps you locked into the ecosystem — which is obviously the whole point.
Now for some design news. Meta released Muse Image this week, its new in-house AI image generator. It's hitting number two on the Arena leaderboards, right behind OpenAI. But here's the friction — it's already causing privacy pushback because you can tag someone's profile and use their public Instagram photos to generate new images without their explicit consent. Meta says people can disable it in settings, but that's basically asking permission after the fact. It's exactly the kind of feature that feels clever until you realise you're in someone else's generated photo.
Last one — there's a startup called General Intuition that's raised $320 million at a $2.3 billion valuation, backed by Bezos and others. They reckon gaming data is better training material for physical AI than internet data. The bet is that video game footage — with all the spatial reasoning and movement baked in — teaches AI models how to actually understand the world in three dimensions. They've already got a quadrupedal robot running on their foundation model. It's a different angle on the AGI question.
Quick toolkit check before you go. GitHub Copilot got a bunch of polish this month — agentic browser tools are now on by default, you can run parallel agent sessions side by side, and there's clearer cost visibility across the board. Claude's latest release fixed a bunch of background-session issues that were causing workers to go idle or lose their context mid-task. And oh, before I go — heard this one today: A state trooper pulls over a doctor for speeding. Trooper asks what he does for a living. Doctor says he's a proctologist. Trooper asks what that is, and the doctor says, "I stretch assholes." Trooper looks confused. "You can actually stretch one?" Doctor says, "Certainly — from about six inches all the way to six feet." Trooper asks, "What do you do with a six-foot asshole?" Doctor replies, "You give him a radar gun and park him beside the highway." Righto, catch you tomorrow.

item.title: AI UX Porridge - 2026-07-08
item.description:
Wednesday, 8 July. It's also National Video Game Day, so fire up whatever you've got handy.
First up — Meta's just launched Muse Image, a new AI image generator that's already causing a stir. It's free on Meta AI, Instagram Stories, and WhatsApp, and honestly, it's landed pretty high on the leaderboards. But here's the thing: you can now use it to manipulate other people's Instagram photos without their knowledge. If someone's got a public profile, you can tag them and create AI images using their pictures. Meta says there's a setting to opt out, but you won't get notified if someone does it. Privacy landmine territory.
Next one — Discord's admitted a pretty nasty bug in its AI moderation system. Over the past two months, more than 8,000 users got wrongfully banned because harmless images — spreadsheets, chessboards, game textures, white backgrounds — were flagged as harmful. The bug's been fixed and accounts are being restored, but it's a solid reminder of how fragile these automated systems are when you're relying on them to make bans permanent. They're working on better safeguards.
Now for some design news. There's a really solid piece on designing in regulated industries — fintech, insurance, healthcare — where compliance isn't the enemy of good design, it's just another constraint. The writer's point is spot on: every great design lives within constraints. Treat legal requirements the same way you'd treat a technical limitation. It actually forces better thinking.
Last one — Anthropic's published research on something they're calling J-space: basically an internal workspace in Claude that emerged on its own during training. It's where the model does its actual thinking, separate from the chain-of-thought text it writes out. When they edited these internal patterns — swapping "spider" for "ant" — it actually changed the model's answers. Neuroscientists are talking about it because it mirrors how human brains handle conscious thought. Wild stuff.
Quick toolkit check before you go. GitHub Copilot's usage API now breaks review metrics by adoption phase, so you can see if deeper Copilot adoption actually speeds up code review. Mixpanel just launched Mixpanel Agent—ask it anything about your product data in plain language and it'll investigate metric drops for you.
Oh, and before I go—heard this one today. An actor lands his first role in years on Broadway. Director says, "You walk out with a rose, sniff it, and say 'Oh, the smell of my lover.'" Perfect audition, perfect delivery. Crowd goes nuts... but the director's furious. Actor panics: "Did I forget my lines?" Director says, "No—you forgot the rose."
Righto, have a good one.

item.title: AI UX Porridge - 2026-07-07
item.description:
Tuesday, July 7th, 2026.
First up, Meta's making a big push in the model wars. The company's training something called Watermelon, and according to their AI chief Alexandr Wang, it's already matching OpenAI's GPT-5.5 on benchmarks. Thing is, Watermelon's running on about 10 times the compute of Meta's last model, and it's still in training. The frontier keeps moving though — OpenAI's apparently prepping 5.6 models for release next week, so Meta's chasing a target that keeps getting faster.
Next one's a bit of a mess. Anthropic launched Claude Fable last week, which was supposed to be their Mythos-class model for general use. But just three days later, the US government spooked over a jailbreak found in Fable suspended access for all foreign nationals. Anthropic couldn't make that work cleanly, so they've basically turned the whole thing off for everyone. It's a reminder that frontier models are getting complicated on the policy side, not just the technical side.
Now for some design news. There's been a real shift in how design systems work now that AI's writing so much of our UI. For years, design systems were a consistency tool — nice to have when budgets allow. But when most of your UI's generated by agents rather than written by humans, the game changes. Agents don't remember your guidelines. They don't absorb conventions over time. Every generation starts from scratch, so a weak design system isn't just sloppy — it's actively working against you. That means design systems have become less of an optional consistency layer and more of the actual guardrail keeping generated code coherent.
Last one's about understanding. As agents write more code for us, a lot of people are asking: do we even need to understand what they're building? The answer's yes, but not for the reason you might think. It's not really about verifying their work anymore — agents are getting better at that. It's about staying an active participant in the creative process. Understanding what the agent's doing across multiple iterations means you can actually shape where it's going, rather than just rubber-stamping what comes out.
Catch you tomorrow.

item.title: AI UX Porridge - 2026-07-06
item.description:
Monday, July 6, 2026.
First up, Amazon's winding down Mechanical Turk. From July 30th, they won't be accepting new customers, though existing ones can keep using it. The crowdsourcing platform, which launched in 2005, let people do small tasks for tiny pay — caption images, label data for AI training, that sort of thing. It's become a weird mirror of modern AI: companies used to build models with human-annotated data, and now workers on the platform are using large language models to complete the tasks themselves. So the whole thing's kind of eating its own tail at this point.
Next one's a bit lighter. Google just dropped a commercial imagining the Founding Fathers writing the Declaration of Independence with Google Workspace. It's got docs, calendar meetings, the works — and yes, Gemini helping brainstorm. The whole thing's tongue-in-cheek, and honestly, it's way more about Google's suite than about AI itself. That said, the video itself has that uncanny AI-generated look, which is probably unintentional timing.
Now for some design news. There's a piece on UX Collective about how we've been thinking about animacy all wrong. A Swedish psychologist back in 1973 put reflective dots on someone's joints, filmed them in a dark room, and people instantly saw a human being walking — no face, no body shape. Just movement. The takeaway: the brain detects "aliveness" through motion way before it looks at facial features. It's reshaping how designers should think about robots and interactive systems, especially at places like industrial automation conferences where everyone's obsessed with making machines look friendly.
All good on the toolkit front — nothing new this week worth flagging. Stay sharp out there.

item.title: AI UX Porridge - 2026-07-04
item.description:
Saturday, 4 July 2026.
First up, there's a new fight brewing in browsers. Google Chrome's still the heavyweight, but Perplexity just launched Comet, an AI browser that summarizes emails and books calendar invites for you. The Browser Company's got Dia in beta doing similar things. Meanwhile, open-source projects like WebBrain let you run AI agents locally in Firefox or Chrome without any data leaving your machine. The browser isn't just a window anymore — it's becoming your assistant.
Next, Sam Altman wants Washington at the table. He's calling for a U.S.-led AI safety forum with real regulatory teeth, modeled on how the IAEA policed atomic energy during the Cold War. On top of that, OpenAI's apparently floated the idea of giving the government a 5% equity stake in the company. Both moves signal that the labs know regulation's coming, and they'd rather shape it themselves than have it forced on them.
Now for some design news. A piece in UX Collective walks through the actual stack that gets design from Figma into working code with AI — and spoiler, it's messier than the demo videos suggest. The real work happens across multiple layers: connecting Figma to Claude via something called an MCP, then routing that to a code generation tool, then fixing what breaks. The demos show you one clean layer. Your project needs three or four stacked together, and nobody tells you that until you're already stuck.
Last one: UX Collective also covered how good user experience might've actually broken the job market. Ten years ago, about 15% of applications landed an interview. Today it's down to 2–3%. Why? LinkedIn's Easy Apply, Indeed's one-click jobs, and AI-generated cover letters drove the friction to nearly zero. From the applicant's side that's brilliant UX. But now recruiters get 300+ applications per opening, so candidates have to spam-apply 30 to 50 times just to get a meeting. Better UX on the applying side made hiring harder on the receiving side.
Quick toolkit check before you go. Claude changed how question dialogs work — they won't auto-continue by default anymore, but you can set an idle timeout in /config if you want them to. That's it. Stay sharp out there.

item.title: AI UX Porridge - 2026-07-03
item.description:
Saturday, July fourth, twenty twenty-six. Let's start with the browser wars. They're not about search anymore. Google and Apple still dominate, but 2026 has brought a wave of AI-powered alternatives—Perplexity's Comet, The Browser Company's Dia—all betting the browser becomes less a window onto the web and more an assistant that gets things done. These aren't just incremental updates. They're rethinking what a browser fundamentally is.
Next: Mark Zuckerberg told Meta staff this week that AI agents haven't progressed as fast as he'd hoped. Meta laid off eight thousand people earlier this year and shuffled another seven thousand into AI groups, betting big on agents replacing human work. But the payoff hasn't materialized yet. Zuckerberg said the upside "hasn't come to fruition yet," though he expects improvements in the next three to six months. Meta's spending one hundred forty-five billion on AI infrastructure this year. That's a massive bet that still hasn't paid dividends.
Third: If you design in Figma and wondered how your work actually gets from design into code with AI, a new breakdown walks through the mess. There's no single pipeline yet. The demos show one clean layer working perfectly. Real work needs three or four layers stacked together, and nobody shows you the stack because that's where it gets messy and half-solved. The confusion comes from treating separate things at different stages as one monolithic problem. Understanding the layers—starting with MCPs, the connections between tools—is where clarity starts.
And finally: Good UX might have broken the job market. Ten years ago, about fifteen percent of applications became interviews. Today that's dropped to two or three percent. Why? Friction disappeared. LinkedIn's Easy Apply, one-click applications, resume autofill, AI-generated cover letters—applying now takes thirty seconds instead of thirty minutes. From the applicant's perspective, that's outstanding UX. From the employer's perspective, it's chaos. They're drowning in applications. Better experience for users created a worse outcome for the system.
Thanks for listening. See you tomorrow.

item.title: AI UX Porridge - 2026-07-02
item.description:
Thursday, July 2nd, 2026.
Privacy-first AI platform Venice AI just hit unicorn status with a $65 million Series A. The startup gives users access to over 200 AI models while keeping their data encrypted and untracked. They're already profitable with $70 million in annualized revenue and serving 3 million active users.
India's Bhavin Turakhia is betting $30 million of his own money that the AI era demands completely redesigned workplace software. His new company, Neo, combines project management, documents, storage, and AI into one platform—designed from scratch for AI, not bolted on afterward. Turakhia says you can't just add AI to old enterprise tools any more than you could turn Nokia parts into an iPhone.
Cloudflare just issued AI companies a September deadline. Starting September 15th, their default settings will block "mixed-use" web crawlers from ad-supported pages, forcing a separation between search crawlers and AI training crawlers. The move signals that website owners want their content discoverable but compensated—and Cloudflare's calling out Google for having unfair access to more training data through its search dominance.
On the design side: AI personality is emerging as a critical design problem. Right now it's an accidental byproduct of training and alignment, but researchers argue it should be deliberately designed like any other interface element. The warmth, hedging, and eagerness of AI assistants all shape how users feel—yet nobody's actually choosing these traits.
And Google's Gemini Spark agent is now available on Mac, with new integrations for Google Tasks, Keep, Canva, and Instacart. The expansion lets Spark handle multi-step workflows across your files and connected services.
That's your Thursday briefing.
